title: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (source-language selection)
summary: aha2705 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan uncorrected OCR encoding derived from Richard Morris's 1873 Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series. The cataloged selection comprises the source-language text of thirty-four Early Middle English prose homilies on printed odd pages 3–219 and the continuous 400-line A Moral Ode on pp. 220–232. It omits the homilies' facing Modern English translations, front matter, notes and back matter, and the separate three-hymn appendix and musical material. Morris states that the selected medieval texts come from Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.52; the manuscript places Poema Morale first at ff. 2r–9v, followed by the homilies at ff. 10r–86r and 87v–88r, whereas Morris reorders the poem as item XXXV. A verified 710-page bound Internet Archive carrier preserves both the unrelated First Series and the complete Second Series, and a verified 216-canvas Trinity IIIF bundle preserves the complete provider-published manuscript inventory, including target UV alternates. The record separates textual, manuscript, editorial, encoding, repository, and image-rights layers; it does not promote Morris's comparison readings into direct manuscript derivation edges where consultation routes are unproved.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2705
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The source file is 334,530 bytes and identifies the U-M item as AHA2705.0001.001.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item AHA2705.0001.001
entities.description: A public OCR-derived electronic representation of Morris's book. It transcribes the title page and selected source-language body, links or marks the 302 page-image sequence, and represents front matter, facing translations, notes, back matter, the hymn appendix, and musical matter chiefly as omitted-page wrappers rather than searchable text.

entities.label: Richard Morris, Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (1873)
entities.description: Morris's EETS edition presents thirty-four Early Middle English homilies with facing Modern English translations, followed by A Moral Ode, notes, and a separate appendix of three hymns with musical and photolithographic material. The aha2705 subject is only the thirty-four source-language homilies and A Moral Ode.

bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century: From the Unique MS. B.14.52 in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, Second Series, with Three Thirteenth-Century Hymns, EETS Original Series 53 (London: N. Trübner, 1873).
bibliographic.title: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series

notes: The title page calls the hymn source MS. 54 D.4.14, while the appendix heading calls it MS. 54, D.5.14; these hymns are outside the aha2705 subject and the discrepancy is not silently resolved.
Morris's comparison notes cite other Poema Morale readings, but the exact route from earlier print to physical witnesses remains unresolved and is not modeled as direct codex consultation.

entities.label: Cambridge, Trinity College MS B.14.52
entities.description: The principal and directly transcribed manuscript witness. Poema Morale occupies ff. 2r–9v; the homilies occupy ff. 10r–86r and, after lexical notes on f. 86v and a blank f. 87r, ff. 87v–88r. The codex is materially and scribally composite, so institutional and scholarly date/origin statements are retained at the witness layer rather than transferred to all component works.

date_statements.label: Relevant copying period in EM1060
date_statements.value: Within 1060–1220, with component-sensitive hand and quire analysis
date_statements.notes: This broader scholarly production window is not a single date for every codicological component.

holding.notes: The current Trinity catalogue has been online since 2022 and provides the official IIIF manifest.

physical_description.extent: 91 folios in the current Trinity summary; the digital labeling and scholarly foliation history include irregular, repeated, stub, and later-numbered surfaces
notes: The published IIIF inventory includes covers, inside covers, flyleaves, two narrow stubs, manuscript leaves, and ten UV alternate images, but no spine or edge views.

entities.label: Internet Archive 710-page bound scan containing Morris's First and Second Series
entities.description: A single bound-carrier PDF. PDF pages 5–398 contain the unrelated First Series; PDF pages 399–710 contain the complete 1873 Second Series. Within that Second Series, the aha2705 source-language homilies occur on printed odd pages 3–219 / PDF pages 433, 435, …, 649, and A Moral Ode on printed pp. 220–232 / PDF pages 650–662.

notes: The facsimile identifier is reused because this is the same physical scan already identified for the 1868 First Series, not a newly invented second digital object.

entities.reproduction_format: Digitized bound-volume page images with OCR text layer

entities.label: Trinity College complete published IIIF inventory for MS B.14.52
entities.description: The complete 216-canvas provider-published inventory. Natural-color target images map Poema Morale to canvases 13–28, homilies I–XXXIII to canvases 31–183, and homily XXXIV to canvases 186–187; canvases 209–212 are UV alternate images of target ff. 9v, 33v, 34r, and 48r.

notes: The ordinary sequence runs from front cover through back cover; later canvases are UV alternates. Complete means the complete published inventory, not exhaustive photography of every physical surface.

scope.notes: The exact transfer and revision history between U-M publication and the pinned repository artifact is not documented in the file.

scope.notes: The manuscript places Poema Morale before the homilies. Morris reorders it after them as XXXV; f. 86v lexical notes and blank f. 87r are not target text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Poema Morale precedes the homilies in Trinity B.14.52; non-target lexical notes and blank surfaces interrupt the last homily sequence.
editorial_practices.representation: Morris prints the thirty-four homilies first and moves Poema Morale after them as XXXV, followed by editorial notes and a separate hymn appendix.
editorial_practices.description: The edition creates an ordered selection rather than mechanically reproducing codex order.
editorial_practices.scope: Selected source-language texts on printed pp. 3–232

editorial_practices.source_feature: Poema Morale comparison readings associated with other witnesses or earlier editions.
editorial_practices.representation: Morris records selected comparative readings in his apparatus.
editorial_practices.description: Comparison evidence is preserved as edition-level collation practice, but no direct relation to Egerton, Lambeth, or Jesus codices is asserted until Morris's consultation routes are established.
editorial_practices.scope: A Moral Ode, printed pp. 220–232 and associated notes
notes: The distinction prevents comparison witnesses from being modeled as homogeneous base-text sources.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The printed book contains title and front matter, facing translations, source-language texts, notes/back matter, an appendix of three hymns, music, and photolithographic material.
editorial_practices.representation: U-M supplies uncorrected OCR for the title page and source-language selection while representing excluded matter chiefly through PB-bearing omitted-section wrappers.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding is a source-language textual selection inside a page-complete digital-image sequence, not a complete searchable transcription of the book.
editorial_practices.scope: Whole U-M item and its 302 PB elements

evidence.citation: Elaine Treharne et al., The Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060 to 1220, Cambridge, Trinity College B.14.52 description.
evidence.locator: summary, contents, hands, codicology, foliation, origin and bibliography
evidence.summary: Academic evidence for the 1060–1220 production frame, southeastern English origin proposals, multiple scribes, self-contained first quire, Poema Morale ff. 2r–9v and 400 lines, homily spans, Latin citations, three foliation systems, and the separated final homily on ff. 87v–88r.

open_questions.question: Did Morris inspect the Egerton, Lambeth, and Jesus Poema Morale codices directly for the comparison readings in the Second Series, or did he carry those readings through his own and others' earlier printed editions?
next_steps: Inspect every comparison siglum and source acknowledgment in Morris's notes and preface.
Compare readings against Morris 1868, Furnivall 1862, Morris 1872, and surviving manuscript images where available.
Add direct collated_against edges only when physical consultation or an exact print route is evidenced.

open_questions.question: What exact source-image package, OCR engine configuration, correction history, and later transformation history produced U-M item AHA2705.0001.001 and the repository XML?
next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and page-image inventory from U-M.
Confirm whether the repository artifact is byte-identical to an archived U-M XML export or reflects later transformations.
Continue to treat the file's explicit no-corrections declaration as the strongest item-specific production evidence.

open_questions.question: Why does Morris's title page identify the separately appended hymn source as MS. 54 D.4.14 while the appendix heading reads MS. 54, D.5.14?
next_steps: Check the current Corpus Christi College catalog and Morris's errata or correspondence.
Keep the three hymns outside aha2705 and preserve both printed forms until the discrepancy is resolved.

open_questions.question: How should the current Trinity page's abbreviated homily list, which appears to omit Morris's XVIII while still describing the manuscript as printed by Morris, be reconciled with the edition and XML's thirty-four homilies?
next_steps: Compare the current web transcription against James's printed catalogue entry and every manuscript rubric.
Do not reduce the target count: the edition, XML, and physical loci support thirty-four prose homilies plus A Moral Ode.

review_notes: The medieval component works, Trinity witness, Morris edition, U-M OCR item, repository XML, Internet Archive scan, and Trinity images are separate entities with separate dates, responsibilities, access paths, and rights records.
The cataloged subject is an edition-defined source-language selection: thirty-four prose homilies plus one verse item. It is not the complete 1873 volume and not a single medieval work.

Morris moves Poema Morale from the beginning of the manuscript to item XXXV after the homilies. The transcribes relation therefore records selected loci and reordered presentation rather than implying identical sequence.

The complete bound PDF and complete Trinity bundle both physically contain the target; each local copy explicitly declares target_work_presence present and maps the narrower work portion inside the whole cached object.
The 710-page PDF is a single carrier containing both series. Reusing facsimile:ia:morris-1868 preserves object identity even though this record's target lies in the Second Series component.

The Trinity bundle contains every one of the provider's 216 published canvases, including covers, flyleaves, two stubs and ten UV alternates. No spine or edge views are exposed, so complete means complete published inventory rather than exhaustive physical-object photography.

Morris's comparison readings are recorded as edition-level collation practice only. No Egerton, Lambeth, or Jesus manuscript entity or direct derivation edge is introduced without evidence of the consultation route.
The U-M XML's omitted back matter wrapper begins with printed p. 218, which is still the final facing translation of homily XXXIV; actual editorial notes/back matter begin on printed p. 233.
The title-page D.4.14 and appendix D.5.14 hymn shelfmarks are preserved as a conflict. The appended hymns have no direct connection with the homilies and are outside the target selection.

The Trinity image license is CC BY-NC 4.0; the ignored cache is a research copy and does not authorize commercial use. Internet Archive metadata supplies no scan-layer rights or license field, so that layer remains unknown rather than inheriting the edition's public-domain status.
